citations.narrative:
On November 15, 2025, the licensee hosted an event at his facility where animal encounters were allowed. A video
posted on social media of the event showed a brown capuchin/tufted capuchin sitting on the shoulder of a young child. A
second video showed the capuchin walking across the shoulders of an adult male attendee. The second video also
showed a female attendee holding a black-and-white ruffed lemur with its face in front of her face. Both videos were
reviewed with the licensee who confirmed that they were his animals and these encounters occurred at his hosted event.
Allowing nonhumane primates to climb on the heads, shoulders, or be face to face with members of the public increases
the risk of injury to both the animals and public. There is also a risk for the spread of diseases.
During public exhibition, any animal must be handled so there is minimal risk of harm to the animal and to the public, with
sufficient distance and/or barriers between the animal and the general viewing public so as to assure the safety of animals
and the public.
Correct by 2/25/2026
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the licensee.
n

narrative:
2.131(c)(1) 
   
Handling of animals. 
On November 15, 2025, the licensee hosted an event at his facility where animal encounters were allowed. A video 
posted on social media of the event showed a brown capuchin/tufted capuchin sitting on the shoulder of a young child. A 
second video showed the capuchin walking across the shoulders of an adult male attendee. The second video also 
showed a female attendee holding a black-and-white ruffed lemur with its face in front of her face. Both videos were 
reviewed with the licensee who confirmed that they were his animals and these encounters occurred at his hosted event.  
 
Allowing nonhumane primates to climb on the heads, shoulders, or be face to face with members of the public increases 
the risk of injury to both the animals and public. There is also a risk for the spread of diseases. 
 
During public exhibition, any animal must be handled so there is minimal risk of harm to the animal and to the public, with 
sufficient distance and/or barriers between the animal and the general viewing public so as to assure the safety of animals 
and the public. 
 
Correct by 2/25/2026 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the licensee. 
 
 n
